question: How are leg ulcers explicitly defined according to the course presentation?
options: Deep dermal tear injuries specifically caused by blunt force trauma impacts
Open sores or wounds that occur on the lower extremities, often due to poor circulation
Localized damage to the stratum corneum due to un-blanchable moisture maceration
Profound necrosis occurring after the complete collapse of arterial oxygen perfusion

question: Which of the following descriptions accurately matches the clinical presentation of a 'Venous Ulcer'?
options: Usually found on the outer side of the ankle; deep, punched-out, and very painful
Usually found below the knee and on the inner ankle; often painless unless infected
Found primarily on the toe tips, displaying a cold, hairless, and pale configuration
Found in deep gluteal folds following prolonged sedentary pressure cycles • • • • Vascular & Tissue Viability • Exam Paper 9 of 20 Page 1 of 7

question: Arterial ulcers result from inadequate blood supply. What specific visual and symptomatic traits are they associated with?
options: "Punched out" appearance, painful, deep wounds, cool affected area, pale when elevated
Brown/black stained skin, intense itching, and weeping exudate
Superficial abrasion tracking following mild friction against bedding
Indistinct borders with thickened hyperpigmented skin zones

question: What is the fundamental pathophysiology behind the development of venous disease as described in the slides?
options: Fatty deposit plaque build-up narrowing the arterial wall pathways
Valve dysfunction causing blood to pool in the lower extremities and fluid to leak into surrounding tissues
Failure of the epidermis to manufacture vitamin D after UV exposure
Excessive production of viscous protein-heavy sweat triggered by emotional strain

question: Atherosclerosis is the causative mechanism for which specific leg condition?
options: Venous insufficiency
Peripheral arterial disease
Skin tear avulsion
Chronic pruritus

question: The pathophysiology slide states that prolonged ischaemia (reduced blood flow) eventually leads to what clinical outcome?
options: Rapid proliferation of collagen type III
Tissue necrosis (tissue death)
Increased capillary permeability and oedema
Enhanced epithelial resurfacing Vascular & Tissue Viability • Exam Paper 9 of 20 Page 2 of 7

question: What clinical observation suggests a venous origin in the symptom list on Slide 5?
options: Deep pain when dangling the leg
Brown or black stained skin
Tight, hairless shiny skin
"Punched-out" wound edges

question: Which of the following is considered a primary symptom of arterial disease?
options: Itchy, hardened skin surfaces
Leg pain at night
General swelling and aching
Scabbing or flaking of the inner ankle

question: What diagnostic classification describes ulcers that display a combination of venous and arterial factors?
options: Mixed Ulcers
Complex Tears
Secondary Intent wounds
Lymphoedematous lesions

question: What is the significance of the "Valve Dysfunction" mentioned in the pathophysiology of venous disease?
options: Valves fail to close properly, causing blood to pool in the lower extremities
Valves over-pressurize, pushing arterial blood directly into the bone marrow
Valves become calcified, preventing any oxygenation of the papillary dermis
Valves prevent the return of interstitial fluid to the lymph system entirely Vascular & Tissue Viability • Exam Paper 9 of 20 Page 3 of 7

question: What happens when arterial plaque buildup narrows the arterial walls?
options: It increases blood flow efficiency
It reduces oxygen and nutrient delivery to tissues, causing ischaemia
It triggers immediate tissue regeneration in the proliferative phase
It causes the immediate transition of collagen from type I to type III

question: Which of the following is defined as the cause of venous insufficiency-related inflammation and oedema?
options: The pooling of blood increases pressure, leading to fluid leakage into surrounding tissues
The rapid production of high-viscosity protein-lipid sweat
The invasion of fungal digestive enzymes that dissolve structural keratin
The formation of arterial fatty plaques that block capillary inflow

question: What clinical sign on Slide 5 is noted as a result of venous disease?
options: Redness when dangling the leg
Discharge
Cool/cold affected area
Deep, punched-out wounds

question: What explicit statement is made regarding the treatment of mixed ulcers?
options: They should only be treated as venous ulcers regardless of arterial history
Treatment varies based on the underlying cause, addressing blood flow, wound care, and risk factors
They require surgical amputation of the extremity within 24 hours of diagnosis
They are resistant to all forms of dressing management and should be left uncovered Vascular & Tissue Viability • Exam Paper 9 of 20 Page 4 of 7

question: Which statement accurately describes the blood flow changes in arterial disease?
options: The leg turns red when elevated and pale when dangling
Redness when dangling the leg, turning pale when elevated
No significant color change is associated with positional elevation
The skin becomes permanently darkened and stained brown across all positions

question: What anatomical location is typically associated with Venous Ulcers?
options: The outer side of the ankle and feet
Around the ankles
The tips of the toes
The deep gluteal sacral crease

question: Why is proper assessment crucial for mixed ulcers?
options: Because they only respond to antibiotic irrigation
For accurate diagnosis and treatment
Because mixed ulcers have no identifiable underlying vascular cause
Because they are highly contagious to adjacent staff members

question: What symptom does a venous ulcer patient usually experience regarding skin feeling?
options: Tight, hairless, and smooth feeling
Itchy, hardened skin
Freezing cold feeling
Deep numbness and complete loss of sensory touch Vascular & Tissue Viability • Exam Paper 9 of 20 Page 5 of 7

question: What is the appearance of a venous ulcer described as?
options: "Punched out" and deep
Surface lesions with indistinct borders and discharge
White, dry necrotic craters
Small, black, dry eschar plugs

question: Which of the following is associated with the arterial ulcer appearance?
options: Scabbing or flaking
Red, yellow, or black sores
Brown stained skin
Excessive weeping exudate

question: What anatomical location is typically associated with Arterial Ulcers?
options: Inner ankle
Outer side of the ankle, feet, heels, or toes
Upper calf muscles
Sacral prominence

question: What causes venous ulcers specifically?
options: Insufficient blood return to the heart due to vein dysfunction
Blocked arteries preventing oxygen reach
Fungal digestive enzyme consumption
Prolonged vertical pressure from wheelchairs Vascular & Tissue Viability • Exam Paper 9 of 20 Page 6 of 7

question: What causes arterial ulcers specifically?
options: Fluid leakage into tissues
Reduced blood flow due to inadequate nutrient-rich blood reaching tissue
Infection via Staphylococcus aureus
Chronic inflammation from poor hygiene

question: Which of the following is associated with the symptom of a venous ulcer?
options: Cool or cold affected area
Aching
Punched out appearance
Redness when dangling the leg

question: Which of the following is an accurate statement about mixed ulcer treatment?
options: Treatment is always identical to arterial ulcer management
Addressing blood flow issues is essential for healing
Mixed ulcers cannot be healed
They do not require wound care
